T1A01: §97.1 lists five purposes for the Amateur Radio Service, and "advancing skills in the technical and communication phases of the art" is one of them word for word — the service exists to build skilled operators and technicians, not just to give people a personal radio channel.
T1A02: Part 97 is a federal regulation, and the FCC is the federal agency that writes and enforces it. The ARRL, by contrast, is a private membership organization with no regulatory authority.
T1A03: §97.119(b)(2) says a phonetic alphabet is "encouraged" for phone identification, not required — it's a best practice for clarity, not a legal mandate you can be cited for skipping.
T1A04: The FCC stopped mailing paper license documents; per §97.23 your official notification is an email containing a link to download your grant from the ULS database, not a certificate in the mail.
T1A05: Your proof of licensure isn't a document at all — it's that your grant actually appears in the FCC's own ULS database (§97.7). If it's not there yet, you aren't licensed, no matter what a CSCE says.
T1A06: §97.203(d) carves out 28.200–28.300 MHz specifically for automatically controlled digital stations (like APRS or PACTOR gateways) on 10 meters — a narrow, purpose-built slice for unattended operation.
T1A07: §97.3(a)(41) defines a space station as an amateur station located more than 50 km above the Earth's surface — that specific altitude is what triggers the special satellite-operation rules elsewhere in Part 97.
T1A08: A Frequency Coordinator (§97.3(a)(22)) is a volunteer who recommends repeater and auxiliary station channels to reduce interference between coordinated systems — not an FCC office, and not a licensing requirement.
T1A09: Frequency Coordinators aren't appointed by the FCC — they're selected by the amateurs actually operating repeaters in a given local or regional area, which is why coordination is a community-run system.
T1A10: RACES stations need something beyond the amateur operator license itself: certification of current enrollment by a civil defense agency (§97.407(a)) — the one extra hurdle that separates RACES from ARES, which needs no outside certification.
T1A11: §97.101(d) prohibits willful or malicious interference outright, with no exceptions — it's one of the few things in Part 97 that's flatly banned regardless of who's "right" about a frequency.
T1B01: §97.301(e) sets Technician's 10-meter phone/image segment at 28.300–28.500 MHz — the upper half of the 0.5 MHz slice, with data/RTTY (plus CW everywhere) below it.
T1B02: Satellite operation just needs the amateur license class the satellite's uplink requires (§97.301, §97.207(c)) — there's no separate NASA or space-agency approval process for a ham to use an amateur satellite.
T1B03: 52.525 MHz falls inside the 6-meter band (50–54 MHz) — the other choices are outside that range entirely, which is really a wavelength/frequency band-boundary check.
T1B04: 146.52 MHz sits inside 144–148 MHz, the 2-meter band — it's actually the nationally recognized 2m FM simplex calling frequency, which is why this exact number shows up on the exam.
T1B05: Technician gets HF privileges on 10 meters *and* VHF/UHF privileges from 6 meters up (§97.301, §97.305) — both are true simultaneously, which is why "all these choices" is correct rather than picking just one band.
T1B06: Technician's only HF phone privilege is on the 10-meter band (28.300–28.500 MHz); there's no phone privilege at all on 80/40/20/15 meters for this license class.
T1B07: §97.305(a),(c) restricts 50.0–50.1 MHz and 144.0–144.1 MHz to CW only — these are the narrow "CW sub-bands" set aside at the bottom edge of the 6m and 2m bands specifically for weak-signal Morse work.
T1B08: In a secondary allocation, amateurs share the band with a primary service that has priority (§97.303) — hams may encounter those primary stations there and must avoid interfering with them, with no "first come, first served" protection.
T1B09: §97.101(a) and 97.301(a-e) combine here: transmitting right at a band edge risks going outside your authorized range from sideband splatter, transmitter drift, or your own display's calibration error — all three are real risks, which is why "all these choices" is the answer.
T1B10: §97.305(c) authorizes SSB phone in at least some segment of every amateur band above 50 MHz — it's not confined to just one or two of them.
T1B11: §97.313 caps Technician's HF operation at 200 watts PEP — well under the 1500 W ceiling that applies to General/Extra operators on the same 10-meter segments.
T1B12: §97.313(b) sets the general amateur power ceiling at 1500 W PEP, which applies except where a specific rule (like Technician's 200 W HF cap) restricts it further.
T1C01: §97.9(a) and 97.17(a) name the three license classes the FCC currently issues: Technician, General, and Amateur Extra. Novice and Advanced still exist for people who already hold them, but you can't newly earn those two anymore.
T1C02: §97.19 lets *any* licensed amateur apply for a vanity call sign, not just General/Extra operators — vanity access isn't gated by license class the way frequency privileges are.
T1C03: §97.117 permits international communications that are incidental to the purposes of the Amateur Radio Service (skill-building, technical exchange, emergency prep) — not communications for personal business or general-purpose chat with no amateur-radio purpose.
T1C04: Per §97.23, your license depends on the FCC being able to reach you — if email contact fails because your address on file is wrong, the rules allow revocation of the station license or suspension of the operator license.
T1C05: Technician Group D call signs follow the 2x3 format (two letters, one digit, three letters) drawn from the K, N, or W prefix pool — KF1XXX matches that pattern.
T1C06: §97.5(a)(2) and 97.11(a) allow operation from a US-documented vessel or aircraft outside the US (and its territorial waters) as long as no foreign country's rules are being violated — your US license travels with you under those conditions.
T1C07: §97.21 and §1.949 allow renewal starting 90 days before your license's expiration date — early enough to avoid any gap, but not so early it can be renewed the whole term ahead.
T1C08: §97.25 sets a flat 10-year term for every amateur license, regardless of class — Technician, General, and Extra all renew on the same 10-year clock.
T1C09: §97.21(a)(b) gives a 2-year grace period after expiration to renew before the license is gone for good and the call sign becomes available to others.
T1C10: Passing the exam only gets you a CSCE, not operating authority — per §97.5a, you may transmit as soon as your grant actually shows up in the FCC's license database (ULS), which is usually within a few business days, not immediately at the test session.
T1C11: During the grace period, the license is expired, not active — §97.21(b) means you must wait for the FCC to actually process the renewal grant before you may operate again, even though the call sign is still reserved for you.
T1D01: §97.111(a)(1) prohibits communicating with any country whose government has formally notified the ITU that it objects to such contacts — a short, specific list, not a broad ban on international contacts generally.
T1D02: One-way transmissions are allowed for several specific purposes (beacons, remote control, etc.), but broadcasting — one-way transmissions intended for the general public — is exactly the case §97.113(b)/97.111(b) prohibit.
T1D03: Obscuring a transmission's meaning is normally forbidden, with one narrow carve-out in §97.211(b)/97.215(b)/97.113(a)(4): sending control commands to space stations or radio-controlled craft, where the whole point is a coded command, not a plain-language message.
T1D04: Music is normally off-limits, but §97.113(a)(4)/(c) allows it when it's incidental to an authorized retransmission — for example, relaying audio from a space shuttle or ISS broadcast that happens to include background music.
T1D05: §97.113(a)(3)(ii) allows notifying other amateurs that equipment is for sale, as long as it's occasional and not run as a regular business — the rule is about not turning the amateur bands into a commercial marketplace.
T1D06: §97.113(a)(4) bans obscene or indecent language outright — there's no context (net, emergency, casual chat) that makes it acceptable.
T1D07: §97.113(d)/97.201(e) defines an auxiliary station as one that sends one-way (or point-to-point) transmissions between a repeater and its associated control/receiver points — infrastructure links, not contacts with other hams.
T1D08: §97.113(a)(3)(iii) lets a control operator permit an unlicensed person to speak on the air as part of classroom instruction, under the control operator's direct supervision and responsibility.
T1D09: Broadcast-related support transmissions are allowed specifically when they're directly related to the immediate safety of human life or protection of property (§97.113(5)(b)) — the same emergency exception that shows up elsewhere in Part 97, not a general "help the media" allowance.
T1D10: §97.3(a)(10) defines broadcasting as transmissions intended for reception by the general public — that's the specific legal meaning that makes one-way public-facing transmissions the type of "broadcasting" §97.113 prohibits.
T1D11: §97.215 exempts transmissions used only to control a model craft (like a radio-controlled plane) from the normal identification requirement — there's no voice or code content to identify in the first place.
T1D12: §97.119(a) requires identifying the transmitting station even during on-the-air test transmissions — "just testing" isn't an exemption from ID rules.
T1E01: §97.7(a) is unconditional: an amateur station may never transmit without a control operator. There's no unattended-operation exception to this one.
T1E02: Per §97.301/97.207(c), the control operator of a station working through a satellite just needs the license class that satellite's uplink requires — any amateur meeting that privilege level may operate it, no separate satellite authorization needed.
T1E03: §97.103(b) puts the responsibility for designating a control operator on the station licensee — it's the licensee's call who operates under their license.
T1E04: §97.103(b) ties frequency privileges to whoever is acting as control operator at the moment, not to whose station license is on file — a higher-class control operator can use their own privileges on someone else's station.
T1E05: §97.3(a)(14) defines the control point as the location where the control operator actually performs the control function (where the microphone or key is) — which can be physically separate from where the transmitter itself sits.
T1E06: Outside of a genuine emergency involving immediate safety of life or protection of property, a Technician may never transmit outside their assigned frequency privileges (§97.301) — "at no time" is the answer because there's no routine exception.
T1E07: §97.103(a) makes both the control operator and the station licensee jointly responsible for proper operation when they're different people — responsibility doesn't shift entirely to one or the other.
T1E08: Repeater operation is the textbook example of automatic control (§97.3(a)(6), 97.205(d)): the station retransmits without a control operator actively keying each individual transmission.
T1E09: §97.109(c) allows any amateur station to be remotely controlled — there's no license-class restriction on which stations qualify.
T1E10: §97.3(a)(39) defines remote control as operating a station via a control link from somewhere other than the station location — operating your home station's radio over the internet from another city is the everyday example.
T1E11: §97.3(a)(13): the control operator is simply the amateur the station licensee designates to be responsible for the station's transmissions — that designation, not physical presence, is what makes someone the control operator.
T1F01: §97.103(c) requires the station and its records be made available for FCC inspection at any time upon request from an FCC representative — not just during a scheduled visit.
T1F02: §97.119(a) requires identification at least every 10 minutes during a contact, and again at the end — not at the start of every single transmission.
T1F03: Same rule as T1F02: §97.119(a)'s 10-minute identification interval applies during the contact and at its conclusion, regardless of how many individual transmissions happen in between.
T1F04: §97.119(b)(2) requires English for identification purposes on phone emissions — the rest of a contact's content isn't restricted this way, but your call sign ID specifically must be in English.
T1F05: §97.119(a) allows call sign identification on a data emission to be sent via CW or phone instead — you don't need a data-mode-native ID method if your radio/software doesn't support one.
T1F06: §97.119(c) permits self-assigned indicators (like /M for mobile or /R for rover) attached to your call sign — several formats are acceptable, which is why "all these choices" is correct here.
T1F07: §97.115(a)(2) restricts third-party communications with a foreign station to countries that have a third-party traffic agreement with the US — without that agreement, the foreign station can't legally relay a third party's message.
T1F08: §97.3(a)(47) defines third-party communications as a message passed by the control operator on behalf of another person (the "third party") who isn't a licensed control operator themselves — the amateur transmits, the message is someone else's.
T1F09: §97.3(a)(40): a repeater station receives and simultaneously retransmits on a different frequency — the "simultaneous, two-frequency" mechanic is exactly what defines it as a repeater rather than some other kind of station.
T1F10: §97.205(g) holds the control operator of the *originating* station accountable if a repeater inadvertently retransmits a prohibited communication — the repeater itself isn't the responsible party for content it didn't originate.
T1F11: §97.5(b)(2) requires a club station license to have at least four members — a smaller group doesn't meet the FCC's definition of a club for licensing purposes.
T2A01: ±600 kHz is the standard 2-meter repeater offset — a fixed band convention, not something you calculate, which is why radios just need to know "plus" or "minus" split plus this one number.
T2A02: 146.520 MHz is the nationally recognized 2-meter FM simplex calling frequency — the agreed-upon spot hams monitor and call CQ on when not using a specific repeater.
T2A03: ±5 MHz is the standard 70-centimeter repeater offset — much larger than 2 meters' 600 kHz because it's a completely separate band convention, not derived from any formula.
T2A04: When you already know who you're calling, lead with their call sign so they know immediately the transmission is meant for them, then identify yourself — the reverse order from a cold CQ.
T2A05: Answering a CQ follows the same "their call sign first" logic: say the calling station's call sign, then your own, so they instantly recognize the reply is for them.
T2A06: The conventional CQ pattern — "CQ" a few times, then "this is," then your call sign — is a widely recognized structure so any listening station immediately understands you're calling generally, not answering someone specific.
T2A07: Repeater offset is simply defined as the difference between a repeater's transmit and receive frequencies — the "split" your radio has to replicate to access it.
T2A08: CQ has no hidden meaning or acronym expansion — it's just the internationally recognized procedural signal for "calling any station, is anyone listening."
T2A09: Announcing your own call sign followed by "listening" is the customary way to invite a contact on a repeater without running a full CQ — a lighter-weight "I'm here if anyone wants to talk" signal.
T2A10: A band plan is a voluntary, amateur-coordinated layer on top of the FCC's hard legal privileges — it organizes which mode or activity customarily uses which slice of a band, but isn't individually enforceable by the FCC the way §97.301 privileges are.
T2A11: Simplex means transmitting and receiving on the same single frequency — both stations hear each other exactly where they're each transmitting, with no repeater or frequency split involved.
T2B01: The reverse function temporarily swaps your radio to listen on the repeater's input (receive) frequency instead of its output — useful for checking whether you can hear the other station directly, without the repeater's help.
T2B02: CTCSS (Continuous Tone-Coded Squelch System) rides a continuous sub-audible tone under your voice the whole time you transmit — the repeater's receiver listens for that specific tone before it opens its squelch.
T2B03: A linked repeater network wires multiple repeaters together (often over the internet) so whatever one receives, all of them retransmit — extending coverage well beyond what any single repeater's antenna site could reach alone.
T2B04: "Can hear it but can't access it" has multiple independent possible causes — mismatched offset, wrong CTCSS tone, or wrong DCS code — and the symptom alone doesn't tell you which, so "all these choices" is correct.
T2B05: Talking too loudly overdrives the microphone, pushing FM deviation past what the receiver's filters cleanly pass — that's what causes audio to distort or drop out specifically on voice peaks.
T2B06: DTMF (Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency) sends two simultaneous tones per keypress — the same system a telephone touch-tone keypad uses — for short commands like dialing an autopatch, not for holding a squelch open continuously.
T2B07: Joining a DMR talkgroup just means programming your radio with that talkgroup's specific ID or code from its code plug — there's no registration process with a person or a Frequency Coordinator involved.
T2B08: With no FCC-enforced priority on a shared simplex frequency, the expected practice when two stations interfere is simply that they negotiate who moves — the same courtesy norm covered earlier in this section.
T2B09: Simplex channels are called out in VHF/UHF band plans specifically so stations within range of each other can communicate directly, without tying up repeater capacity for a contact that doesn't need a repeater's extended range at all.
T2B10: QRM is the Q-signal for "I am receiving interference from other stations" — specifically man-made signal interference, not noise from weather or equipment.
T2B11: QSY is the Q-signal for "I am changing frequency" — compact shorthand inherited from CW/telegraph operating for a common operational statement.
T2B12: DMR's color code (numbered 0–15) is an access code that must be programmed into your radio to match the repeater before it will accept your transmission — a digital gatekeeping mechanism separate from talkgroups.
T2B13: A squelch circuit's whole job is muting your receiver's audio output when no signal is present, so you don't have to listen to constant background hiss between transmissions.
T2B14: A talkgroup is DMR's way of organizing radio traffic into separate logical channels sharing the same physical repeater — so you only hear the conversations relevant to the group you've selected.
T2C01: §97.103(a) means Part 97 rules always apply to an amateur station's operation — there's no special "emergency mode" that suspends them, even during a declared emergency.
T2C02: A Net Control Station's job is to call the net to order and direct who transmits when — not to verify anyone's license, and not to set the meeting time (that's decided in advance by whoever organizes the net).
T2C03: Spelling an unusual or easily-mished word using a standard phonetic alphabet ("Alpha Bravo Charlie…") removes ambiguity that plain speech can't — exactly the tool T1A introduced as encouraged for routine identification, applied here to message accuracy.
T2C04: RACES (Radio Amateur Civil Emergency Service) is a Part 97 amateur service specifically for civil defense communications during national emergencies, requiring certification by a civil defense agency beyond the amateur license itself.
T2C05: In net terminology, "traffic" means formal messages being relayed between stations — not a count of check-ins or a measure of general net activity.
T2C06: ARES (Amateur Radio Emergency Service) is a group of hams who've voluntarily registered their qualifications and equipment for public-service duty — no outside certification required beyond the amateur license, unlike RACES.
T2C07: Standard net etiquette is to transmit only when Net Control directs you to, unless you're reporting an actual emergency — the net's whole point is orderly, directed communication rather than open free-for-all.
T2C08: Winlink relays messages to and from ordinary internet email addresses using amateur call signs as the routing identity — useful when a station has RF access but no direct internet connection.
T2C09: A control operator may transmit outside their normal frequency privileges only in situations involving the immediate safety of human life or protection of property — a narrow emergency exception, not a general allowance during any organized net or exercise.
T2C10: A radiogram's preamble is the routing and tracking information at the top of the message — not the sender's contact information — which lets stations relaying it keep track of where it's been.
T2C11: The "check" in a radiogram header is simply a count of words or word-equivalents in the message text — a lightweight integrity check so a relaying station can confirm nothing was dropped in transit, the same way a checksum verifies a data file.
T2C12: RACES specifically requires certification by a civil defense agency on top of the amateur operator license — the one requirement that separates it from ARES, MARS, and SKYWARN.
T3A01: Multipath means your receiver hears the direct signal plus reflections off the ground, buildings, or hills arriving at slightly different times — those copies reinforce or cancel each other depending on exact path lengths, so moving the antenna by a fraction of a wavelength changes which one happens.
T3A02: Vegetation absorbs UHF and microwave wavelengths specifically — a UHF handheld in dense woods performs measurably worse than the same radio in the open at the same distance, because leaves and branches soak up energy at those short wavelengths.
T3A03: Horizontal is the convention for long-distance weak-signal CW and SSB contacts on VHF/UHF — most repeaters use vertical instead, which is why matching polarization to the kind of contact you're making matters.
T3A04: Cross-polarization (vertical talking to horizontal, or vice versa) reduces received signal strength on a line-of-sight VHF/UHF path — the mismatch between transmit and receive antenna orientation loses signal even when everything else about the path is fine.
T3A05: Aiming a directional antenna at a reflective surface — a building, a hill — can bounce your signal into a repeater or station you can't reach directly, turning multipath from a nuisance into a deliberate tool.
T3A06: Picket fencing is the rapid flutter heard on a mobile FM signal as a moving vehicle continuously sweeps through alternating zones of multipath reinforcement and cancellation.
T3A07: Precipitation specifically — not wind, cold, or pressure — degrades microwave range through rain fade: water droplets are large enough relative to microwave wavelengths to absorb and scatter the signal.
T3A08: Irregular fading on ionospheric HF signals comes from multiple sky-wave paths combining unpredictably — the same multipath mechanism as VHF fading, just happening between different ionospheric reflection paths instead of ground reflections.
T3A09: The ionosphere scrambles a signal's polarization on the way back down (it arrives elliptically polarized), so for HF skywave contacts, either vertically or horizontally polarized antennas can work — neither the original transmit polarization nor a specific matching receive polarization survives the trip.
T3A10: Multipath corrupts a digital signal's timing and amplitude unpredictably rather than simply amplifying or delaying it, so its practical effect on data transmissions is a higher error rate, not something you could "correct" by adjusting speed.
T3A11: The ionosphere is the layered, ionized region of the upper atmosphere that can bend HF radio waves back down to Earth — the mechanism behind routine long-distance HF contacts.
T3A12: 10-meter and 6-meter wavelengths are long enough that small water droplets in fog or rain barely interact with the signal, so those bands see little effect — unlike microwave frequencies, where rain fade is a real problem.
T3B01: A radio wave's electric and magnetic fields oscillate together but always at right angles to each other (and to the direction of travel) — that fixed 90° geometry is a defining property of how electromagnetic waves are structured.
T3B02: Polarization is specifically defined as the orientation of the electric field — the magnetic field is always along for the ride at 90° to it, so there's no separate "magnetic polarization" to track.
T3B03: A radio wave has exactly two components: an electric field and a magnetic field, oscillating together at right angles — nothing else varies as part of the wave itself.
T3B04: In free space, every radio wave travels at the speed of light regardless of frequency — HF, VHF, UHF, and microwaves all move at the identical velocity; only wavelength changes with frequency, not speed.
T3B05: The relationship is inverse: as frequency goes up, wavelength gets shorter, because the same fixed velocity (speed of light) has to fit more oscillation cycles into the same one second.
T3B06: Wavelength in meters equals 300 divided by frequency in MHz — a simplified form of (speed of light) ÷ frequency, with units arranged for convenience so amateur-band frequencies produce clean, recognizable numbers.
T3B07: Amateur bands are named by their approximate wavelength ("10 meters," "2 meters") in addition to frequency, because 300 ÷ frequency(MHz) for each band's center rounds to that name — "10 meters" and "28 MHz" are two labels for the same physical thing.
T3B08: VHF (Very High Frequency) spans 30–300 MHz by definition — the range that contains the 6-meter and 2-meter amateur bands.
T3B09: UHF (Ultra High Frequency) spans 300–3000 MHz by definition — the range that contains 70 centimeters and up.
T3B10: HF (High Frequency) spans 3–30 MHz by definition — the range containing every amateur HF band, including Technician's 10-meter slice.
T3B11: The speed of light in free space is approximately 300,000,000 meters per second — the fixed velocity every radio wave travels at regardless of frequency, and the constant the wavelength formula is built from.
T3B12: All radio frequencies travel at the same velocity in free space — frequency changes wavelength, not speed, so there's no "faster" or "slower" radio frequency.
T3C01: UHF signals usually aren't propagated by the ionosphere the way HF is — they mostly pass straight through it into space, so simplex UHF range is normally limited to roughly the radio horizon rather than getting long-distance ionospheric help.
T3C02: Long-distance ionospheric propagation is far more common and reliable on HF than on VHF/UHF — the defining practical difference between the two ranges for making distant contacts.
T3C03: Auroral backscatter signals reflect off turbulent, shifting ionization in the aurora rather than traveling a direct path, which gives received signals a characteristic distorted, raspy sound — the on-air signature that identifies this mode.
T3C04: Sporadic E is the propagation mode most associated with occasional strong band openings on 10, 6, and 2 meters — dense, patchy E-layer ionization briefly reflecting signals well above what the F layer normally handles.
T3C05: Knife-edge diffraction lets radio waves bend slightly around the sharp edge of an obstruction (a ridgeline, a building edge), reaching a receiver with no true line-of-sight path to the transmitter at all.
T3C06: Tropospheric ducting is responsible for over-the-horizon VHF/UHF contacts — a temperature inversion traps and guides the signal in a duct well past the normal radio horizon, with no ionosphere involved.
T3C07: 6 meters is the band best suited to meteor scatter — signals bounce off the brief ionized trail a meteor leaves burning up, and contacts use short, fast transmissions timed to that narrow window.
T3C08: Tropospheric ducting is caused by temperature inversions in the lower atmosphere — a layer of warm air over cooler air that traps and guides VHF/UHF signals well beyond their normal range.
T3C09: F-region long-distance 10-meter propagation is generally best from dawn to shortly after sunset during periods of high sunspot activity — sunlight drives the ionization the F region needs, and that ionization decays once the sun sets.
T3C10: During high points in the sunspot cycle, both 6 and 10 meters can get F-region long-distance openings — the higher UHF/microwave bands are still too high in frequency for the F region to bend back down, regardless of sunspot activity.
T3C11: The radio horizon for VHF/UHF extends slightly past the visual horizon because the atmosphere refracts (bends) radio waves a small amount as they skim along the curve of the Earth — a modest bonus distinct from true ionospheric skip.
T4A01: 13.8 volts at 12 amperes is the standard rating for a 50 W mobile FM transceiver — 13.8 V matches a vehicle charging system's nominal voltage, and 12 A covers both the RF output and the amplifier's inefficiency plus receiver/control circuitry.
T4A02: An accessory SWR meter has to be rated for the frequency and power level you're actually measuring at — one built for HF won't necessarily read correctly at 2 meters, and a QRP-rated meter won't survive full legal-limit power.
T4A03: Short, heavy-gauge DC power wiring minimizes voltage drop under load — a thin or long run has enough resistance to drop a meaningful chunk of voltage right when current spikes during transmit, a plain Ohm's-law effect, not an RF concern.
T4A04: A digital-mode station connects the transceiver's audio input and output directly to a computer's audio output and input — the computer (running software like FT8) handles the encoding/decoding, not a separate hardware converter box.
T4A05: An RF power meter has to physically sit in the feed line, between the transmitter and the antenna, because it's measuring the RF energy actually flowing toward the antenna.
T4A06: A computer-radio digital mode interface carries exactly three signals: receive audio (radio to computer), transmit audio (computer to radio), and transmitter keying (so software can key the radio without a physical push-to-talk).
T4A07: The computer's "line in" connects to the transceiver's speaker connector — that's the receive-audio path (radio to computer), distinct from the separate transmit-audio and keying connections.
T4A08: Flat copper strap is preferred for RF bonding because it has much more surface area than round wire for the same cross-section, and RF current concentrates near a conductor's surface (skin effect) — more surface area means lower impedance right where it matters at radio frequencies.
T4A09: Battery runtime is the ampere-hour rating divided by the equipment's average current draw, not its peak transmit current — most operating time is spent receiving or between transmissions, so average draw is what actually determines how long the battery lasts.
T4A10: A digital mode hotspot bridges a nearby low-power handheld to a digital voice or data network (usually over the internet), letting it reach a DMR/D-STAR talkgroup or reflector without a full-size repeater anywhere in range.
T4A11: A mobile transceiver's negative power return should connect directly at the vehicle's 12-volt battery chassis ground, giving return current a low-resistance, predictable path back to the source instead of hunting through the car's body panels.
T4A12: An electronic keyer takes paddle motions from the operator and turns them into correctly timed dits and dahs — it assists manual Morse sending rather than requiring every element to be tapped out by hand on a straight key.
T4B01: Excessive microphone gain overdrives the SSB audio stage and distorts the transmitted signal — the voice-mode equivalent of the FM over-deviation problem from T2B.
T4B02: A transceiver's operating frequency can be entered via the keypad (typing a specific frequency) or the VFO knob (tuning continuously by hand) — both are normal, direct frequency-entry methods.
T4B03: To hear a weak FM signal that's having trouble holding the squelch open, set the squelch threshold low enough that receiver audio is on all the time — effectively opening the gate all the way, at the cost of hearing background noise between transmissions.
T4B04: An FM signal received slightly off frequency comes out distorted, not just quieter or uniformly shifted in pitch — FM demodulation depends on accurately tracking the carrier's frequency swings, and a mistuned receiver decodes those swings incorrectly.
T4B05: The scanning function tunes through a range of frequencies automatically, checking each one for activity, so you don't have to step through channels by hand.
T4B06: RIT (Receiver Incremental Tuning), or Clarifier, nudges your receive frequency without moving your transmit frequency — the fix for a station whose voice pitch sounds slightly off, without drifting off the frequency they're listening on.
T4B07: A DMR code plug is configuration data loaded onto your radio — the talkgroups, repeaters, and access codes it knows about — not a physical hardware adapter you insert.
T4B08: A choice of receiver filter bandwidths lets you match the filter to the mode, reducing noise and interference by excluding everything outside the bandwidth the signal actually needs — too narrow clips the signal, too wide lets in unnecessary noise.
T4B09: A specific group of stations is selected on a DMR transceiver by entering that talkgroup's identification code — the same mechanism T2B introduced for joining a talkgroup.
T4B10: 2400 Hz gives the best signal-to-noise ratio for SSB voice among the common filter choices — narrow enough to exclude a good deal of noise, wide enough to pass the frequency range SSB voice actually needs.
T4B11: A D-STAR transceiver must have your call sign programmed in before it will transmit at all, since D-STAR carries identification digitally as part of the signal rather than relying on a spoken ID.
T5A01: Current — the flow of electrons — is measured in amperes, one of the four basic electrical quantities alongside voltage, power, and frequency.
T5A02: Power — the rate at which electrical energy is used — is measured in watts.
T5A03: Current is specifically the term for the flow of electrons through a circuit, the electrical equivalent of water flowing through a pipe.
T5A04: Frequency describes how many times per second an alternating current completes one full cycle, measured in hertz.
T5A05: A difference in voltage between two points is what causes electrons to flow between them, the same way water only flows from a high-pressure point to a low-pressure one.
T5A06: Hertz is the unit of frequency — one hertz equals one cycle per second.
T5A07: Metals are good conductors because they have a large supply of free electrons — electrons loosely enough bound to drift from atom to atom under a voltage push.
T5A08: Glass has very few free electrons available to carry current, which is exactly why it's used as an insulator.
T5A09: Alternating current reverses direction on a regular cycle, continuously switching between flowing positive and negative — unlike DC, which flows in one direction only.
T5A10: Power is the rate at which electrical energy is used or delivered — how much work the flow is doing per second, not just how much charge exists.
T5A11: Resistance opposes current flow equally regardless of type — DC, AC, or RF — since resistance doesn't care which direction (or how fast) the current is alternating.
T5B01: "Milli" means ÷1,000, so converting from the base unit to milli means multiplying by 1,000: 1.5 × 1,000 = 1,500 milliamperes.
T5B02: Dividing by 1,000 converts hertz to kilohertz: 1,500,000 Hz ÷ 1,000 = 1,500 kHz.
T5B03: "Kilo" means ×1,000, so one kilovolt equals exactly 1,000 volts.
T5B04: "Micro" means ÷1,000,000, so one microvolt equals one one-millionth of a volt.
T5B06: Dividing by 1,000 converts milliamperes to amperes: 3,000 mA ÷ 1,000 = 3 amperes.
T5B07: Multiplying by 1,000 converts MHz to kHz: 3.525 MHz × 1,000 = 3,525 kHz.
T5B08: Picofarads convert to microfarads by dividing by 1,000,000: 1,000,000 pF ÷ 1,000,000 = 1 microfarad.
T5B09: 10 ÷ 5 = 2, an exact doubling of power, and doubling power is always +3 dB regardless of the starting wattage.
T5B10: 12 ÷ 3 = 4, a 4× decrease — "cut in half, then cut in half again": −3 dB + (−3 dB) = −6 dB.
T5B11: 200 ÷ 20 = 10 exactly, a straight 10× increase, which is always +10 dB with no need to break it into doubling steps.
T5B12: Dividing by 1,000 converts kHz to MHz: 28,400 kHz ÷ 1,000 = 28.400 MHz — inside Technician's 10-meter phone segment.
T5B13: Dividing by 1,000 converts MHz to GHz: 2,425 MHz ÷ 1,000 = 2.425 GHz.
T5C01: Capacitance is specifically defined as the ability to store energy in an electric field, typically between two conductive plates separated by an insulator.
T5C02: The farad is the unit of capacitance (commonly seen as microfarads or picofarads, since a full farad is enormous for small-signal circuits).
T5C03: Inductance is the ability to store energy in a magnetic field, typically built from a coil of wire that a current flows through.
T5C04: The henry is the unit of inductance.
T5C05: The ohm is the unit of impedance — the same unit as plain resistance, since impedance is resistance and reactance combined.
T5C06: kHz is the standard abbreviation for kilohertz.
T5C07: MHz is the standard abbreviation for megahertz.
T5C08: DC electrical power equals current times voltage: P = I × E.
T5C11: Rearranging P = I × E to solve for current: I = P ÷ E = 120 ÷ 12 = 10 amperes.
T5C12: Impedance is the total opposition to AC current flow — resistance and reactance combined — measured in ohms.
T5D01: Ohm's Law rearranged to solve for current: I = E ÷ R.
T5D02: Ohm's Law rearranged to solve for voltage: E = I × R.
T5D03: Ohm's Law rearranged to solve for resistance: R = E ÷ I.
T5D13: In a series circuit, there's only one path for electrons, so current is the same everywhere in the loop — like a single-lane road where every car passes every point.
T5D14: In a parallel circuit, every component connects across the same two points, so every component sees the same voltage even though current can split differently through each branch.
T6A01: A resistor is the component that opposes the flow of current in a DC circuit, converting some of the electrical energy into heat.
T6A02: A potentiometer is a resistor with a movable third contact (a "wiper") that lets you dial resistance up or down by turning a knob — exactly why it's the standard adjustable volume control.
T6A03: Turning a potentiometer's knob or slider directly changes resistance, which is what changes how much signal (like audio) gets through.
T6A04: A capacitor stores energy in an electric field, built from two conductive plates separated by an insulating dielectric.
T6A05: A capacitor is physically just two conductive surfaces (plates) separated by an insulator — no direct electrical path between them, just an electric field across the gap.
T6A06: An inductor stores energy in a magnetic field, the magnetic counterpart to a capacitor's electric-field storage.
T6A07: An inductor is typically built as nothing more than a coil of wire — current flowing through it builds up the magnetic field where the energy lives.
T6A08: An SPDT (single-pole double-throw) switch routes a single circuit between one of two other circuits — a selector connecting one input to either of two possible destinations.
T6A09: Figure T-2's component 3 is drawn as a basic single-throw toggle — an SPST (single-pole single-throw) power switch in series with the battery and fuse.
T6A10: NiMH, Li-ion, and lead-acid are all rechargeable chemistries in common ham use — every choice listed is rechargeable, so "all these choices" is correct.
T6A11: Carbon-zinc is a classic non-rechargeable (single-use) chemistry, chemically similar to an alkaline cell — once depleted, it's thrown away rather than recharged.
T6B01: Every diode drops a small forward voltage whenever current flows through it, and that drop genuinely differs between diode types — a lower forward drop isn't a defect, just a property of that diode's construction.
T6B02: A diode is the semiconductor component that allows current to flow in only one direction, blocking the reverse direction.
T6B03: A transistor can act as an electronic switch, turning a larger current on or off using a much smaller control signal, with no mechanical moving contacts.
T6B04: A transistor can be built from three regions of semiconductor material — that three-region structure is what gives it both switching and amplifying ability, unlike a diode's simpler two-terminal structure.
T6B05: A field-effect transistor (FET) has electrodes named gate, drain, and source — controlled by a voltage on the gate rather than a control current.
T6B06: A diode's cathode lead is usually marked with a stripe printed on the body — the quick visual way to identify it without a datasheet.
T6B07: An LED emits light specifically when forward current flows through it — no light on reverse bias, and no RF coupling involved.
T6B08: FET is simply the abbreviation for Field Effect Transistor.
T6B09: A diode's two leads are named anode and cathode — current flows from anode to cathode when the diode is forward-biased.
T6B10: A transistor can provide power gain — one of the two things (along with switching) that separates it from a simple diode.
T6B11: Gain can be expressed as a ratio of output-to-input voltage, current, or power — all three are valid, equivalent ways of describing the same amplified signal, so "all these choices" is correct.
T6B12: A bipolar junction transistor's three electrodes are named emitter, base, and collector — a small current into the base controls a much larger current from collector to emitter.
T6C01: A schematic is the term for an electrical diagram that represents components using standard symbols rather than realistic drawings.
T6C02: Component 1 in figure T-1 is the zig-zag symbol between the input jack and the transistor — a resistor.
T6C03: Component 2 in figure T-1 is the circled symbol with the angled arrow lead — the transistor that controls current to the lamp circuit.
T6C04: Component 3 in figure T-1 is the rounded dome symbol at the top — a lamp that lights when the transistor switches on.
T6C05: Component 4 in figure T-1 is drawn as stacked long and short parallel lines — the standard battery symbol, supplying the lamp circuit.
T6C06: Component 6 in figure T-2 is the parallel-plate symbol after the rectifier diode — a capacitor, smoothing the pulsing DC into steadier DC.
T6C07: Component 8 in figure T-2 is a diode symbol with light-emission arrows — a light-emitting diode, used here as a "power on" indicator.
T6C08: Component 9 in figure T-2 is a zig-zag with an arrow through it, just before the output — a variable resistor, likely an output-adjustment trimmer.
T6C09: Component 4 in figure T-2 is the paired coil symbols after the switch — a transformer, stepping the AC voltage down for the rectifier stage.
T6C10: Component 3 in figure T-3 is the coiled symbol with a feedback loop heading to ground — a variable inductor, part of the antenna tuner's matching network.
T6C11: Component 4 in figure T-3 is the triangular symbol at the top of the circuit — the antenna the tuner is matching the feed line to.
T6C12: A schematic accurately represents component connections — how the parts wire to each other — not wire lengths or physical appearance.
T6D01: A rectifier changes alternating current into a varying direct-current signal — it makes the current stop reversing direction, without smoothing it (that's the filter capacitor's job).
T6D02: A relay is an electrically-controlled switch: a small control current energizes a coil that mechanically opens or closes a separate set of contacts.
T6D03: Shielded wire prevents unwanted signals from coupling onto (or off of) the conductor it surrounds — a grounded shield intercepts stray electric fields before they induce noise on the signal inside.
T6D04: A meter displays an electrical quantity — voltage, current, power, or SWR — as a numeric value read directly rather than inferred.
T6D05: A regulator is the circuit that controls the amount of voltage coming from a power supply, holding the output steady as input voltage or load current varies.
T6D06: A transformer changes 120 V AC power to a lower (or higher) AC voltage for other uses — the step-down transformer in figure T-2 is a direct example.
T6D07: An LED is commonly used as a simple visual indicator — the "power on" light from figure T-2's power-supply circuit.
T6D08: A capacitor combines with an inductor to form a resonant circuit — the two opposing effects on AC current cancel out at one specific frequency.
T6D09: An integrated circuit combines several semiconductors and other components into one fabricated package — a whole amplifier or logic circuit's worth of parts sold as a single component.
T6D10: Component 2 in figure T-1 (the transistor) controls the flow of current to the lamp downstream — not giving off light itself (that's the lamp) or supplying energy (that's the battery).
T6D11: A resonant (tuned) circuit is simply an inductor and a capacitor wired together in series or parallel — nothing more exotic than that combination is required.
T7A01: Sensitivity is specifically the term for a receiver's ability to detect the presence of a signal at all — how weak a signal it can pull out of the noise floor.
T7A02: A transceiver is a device that combines a receiver and a transmitter in one box, sharing circuitry like the VFO and display between receive and transmit.
T7A03: A mixer is what converts a signal from one frequency to another — feed it two signals and it outputs their sum and difference frequencies.
T7A04: Selectivity is specifically the term for a receiver's ability to discriminate between multiple signals — how well it rejects a strong station sitting right next to the one you're copying.
T7A05: An oscillator is the circuit that generates a signal at one specific frequency — the reference tone everything else gets built from or compared against.
T7A06: A transverter converts the RF input and output of an existing transceiver to another band entirely, rather than converting within one receiver's internal stages.
T7A07: A transceiver's PTT (push-to-talk) input switches it from receive to transmit when grounded — the actual electrical mechanism behind pressing the mic button.
T7A08: Modulation is the term for combining speech with an RF carrier signal — the process that puts your voice onto the transmitted wave.
T7A09: The SSB/CW-FM switch on some VHF power amplifiers sets the amplifier for proper operation in whichever mode you selected — SSB needs linear biasing to preserve the voice waveform, while CW and FM don't.
T7A10: An RF power amplifier is what you add after a transceiver's output specifically to increase the transmitted output power.
T7A11: The Variable Frequency Oscillator (VFO)'s job is setting the transceiver's actual receive and transmit frequency.
T7B01: Talking farther away from the microphone reduces how loud your audio drives the modulator, which is the direct fix for a report that your FM signal is over-deviating.
T7B02: Fundamental overload happens because a nearby broadcast receiver's front end can't reject a strong out-of-band signal — it "hears" your legal, nearby transmission even though you're nowhere near its frequency.
T7B03: Fundamental overload, harmonics, and other spurious emissions can all independently cause radio-frequency interference — every choice listed is a real cause, so "all these choices" is correct.
T7B04: High SWR is a cause of low RF power output from a solid-state transmitter — many radios deliberately reduce power as SWR climbs to protect their output transistors.
T7B05: Blocking the amateur signal with a filter at the affected receiver's own antenna input is the fix for interference your station causes to a non-amateur receiver — the filter belongs on the receiver being overloaded, not on your transmitter.
T7B06: The first step when a neighbor reports interference is to confirm your own station is functioning properly and isn't even interfering with your own radio or TV — before assuming the problem is on their end.
T7B07: A band-reject (notch) filter tuned to the interfering station's frequency, installed at your own 2-meter receiver, keeps a nearby strong signal from overloading your front end.
T7B08: When something in a neighbor's home is causing interference to your station, working with them to identify the device, explaining that FCC rules restrict interference-causing equipment, and making sure your own station meets good practice are all appropriate — "all these choices" is correct.
T7B09: The first troubleshooting step for non-fiber cable-TV interference is mundane: make sure every coax connector in the TV feed line is installed properly — a loose or poorly-shielded fitting is often the entire problem.
T7B10: Being off frequency, talking too loudly or too close to the mic, or operating from a bad location can each independently cause a distorted-audio report — "all these choices" is correct because there's no single universal cause.
T7B11: A clip-on ferrite choke around the microphone cable can eliminate distortion caused by your own transmitted RF sneaking back into the audio path through the cable acting as an unintentional antenna.
T7C01: A dummy load's primary purpose is preventing test transmissions from actually going out over the air — the resistor absorbs the RF power as heat instead of radiating it.
T7C02: An antenna analyzer is used to determine whether an antenna is resonant at the desired operating frequency, without needing a full transmitter and SWR meter setup.
T7C03: A typical RF dummy load is a 50-ohm non-inductive resistor mounted on a heat sink, built to absorb power as heat rather than radiate it.
T7C04: 1:1 SWR indicates a perfect impedance match between antenna and feed line — nothing is reflected back down the line.
T7C05: Solid-state transmitters reduce output power as SWR increases specifically to protect the RF output amplifier transistors from the voltage stresses reflected power causes.
T7C06: An SWR reading of 4:1 indicates an impedance mismatch — a meaningful fraction of your power is reflecting back down the feed line instead of radiating.
T7C07: Power lost in a feed line is converted into heat — the same physics as a dummy load's resistor, just spread out along the length of the cable.
T7C08: A directional wattmeter is the instrument that determines SWR — it separately measures forward and reflected power and computes the ratio from those two numbers.
T7C09: Moisture contamination is a leading cause of coaxial cable failure — water getting into the cable degrades it over time.
T7C10: A coax jacket needs UV resistance because ultraviolet light can damage the jacket and let water enter the cable — the real long-term threat, not the UV interacting with the RF directly.
T7C11: Foam-dielectric coax has less loss per foot than solid-dielectric coax, which is why it's the common choice for long feed line runs.
T7D01: A voltmeter is the instrument used to measure electric potential (voltage).
T7D02: A voltmeter connects in parallel with a component — it taps across two points to measure the difference between them without interrupting the current path.
T7D03: When measuring current, a multimeter connects in series — it has to physically become part of the circuit path, since the current being measured has to flow through the meter.
T7D04: An ammeter is the instrument used to measure electric current.
T7D05: An ohmmeter measures resistance by applying a small current from its own internal battery and measuring the resulting voltage — Ohm's Law run in reverse.
T7D06: Attempting to measure voltage while a multimeter is set to its resistance (ohms) function can damage it — the circuit's own voltage fights the meter's internal battery.
T7D07: A multimeter is commonly used to measure voltage and resistance (along with current) — combining all three functions in one instrument with a selector switch.
T7D08: Acid-core solder should not be used for radio and electronic applications — it's meant for plumbing and leaves a corrosive residue that eats away at fine wires and circuit board traces.
T7D09: A cold solder joint has a characteristic rough or lumpy surface — it never got hot enough to properly flow and bond, unlike a good joint's bright, shiny appearance.
T7D10: An ohmmeter reads increasing resistance over time when connected across a large, discharged capacitor — the meter's own small current is charging the capacitor, so less current flows for the same applied voltage as time passes.
T7D11: Ensuring the circuit is not powered is a necessary precaution before measuring in-circuit resistance — the circuit's own voltage fighting the ohmmeter's internal battery is what causes damage or false readings.
T8A01: SSB is technically a form of amplitude modulation — it's what's left of an AM signal after removing the carrier and one of its two redundant sidebands.
T8A02: FM or PM is the modulation commonly used for VHF packet radio transmissions.
T8A03: SSB is the voice mode of choice for long-distance, weak-signal VHF/UHF contacts, because it packs into much less bandwidth than FM for the same information.
T8A04: FM or PM is what's normally used for VHF/UHF voice repeaters.
T8A05: CW has the narrowest bandwidth of the common signal types — roughly 150 Hz, since it's just on/off keying with no audio to carry.
T8A06: Upper sideband (USB) is the convention used for SSB voice on 10-meter HF and on every VHF/UHF band — the radio defaults to it, no choice needed.
T8A07: SSB signals have narrower bandwidth than FM for the same voice information — the core tradeoff that makes SSB the weak-signal choice.
T8A08: A typical SSB voice signal has a bandwidth of about 3 kHz — roughly matching the audio bandwidth of one sideband of voice.
T8A09: An FM voice signal on VHF repeaters has a bandwidth between 10 and 15 kHz — deliberately spread wider than SSB, trading spectrum for noise immunity.
T8A10: AM fast-scan TV transmissions have a bandwidth of about 6 MHz — a full moving picture needs vastly more information per second than voice.
T8A11: A CW signal requires an approximate bandwidth of 150 Hz — the narrowest of the common modes, since it's just on/off keying.
T8A12: A disadvantage of FM compared to SSB is that only one signal can be received at a time — the capture effect means a receiver locks onto whichever FM signal is stronger and suppresses the rest.
T8B01: Satellite beacons typically transmit the health and status of the satellite — telemetry about the spacecraft itself, not a general propagation beacon.
T8B02: Excessive effective radiated power on a satellite uplink blocks access by other users — one overly strong signal desensitizes the shared transponder for everyone riding through it.
T8B03: Satellite tracking programs provide a ground-track map, pass timing/azimuth/elevation, and Doppler-corrected frequency — all of it, which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T8B04: Amateur radio satellites commonly use FM, SSB, CW, and digital modes — multiple mode types are all in real use, which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T8B05: A satellite beacon is a transmission from the satellite itself that contains status information about the spacecraft.
T8B06: The Keplerian elements — the standard set of numbers describing a satellite's orbit shape, tilt, and position — are the inputs a satellite tracking program needs to work.
T8B07: Doppler shift is an observed change in received signal frequency caused by relative motion between the satellite and your station — the same effect as a passing siren changing pitch.
T8B08: U/V mode means the satellite's uplink is on the 70-centimeter band and its downlink is on the 2-meter band.
T8B09: Spin fading is caused by rotation of the satellite and its antennas — as the spacecraft tumbles, its antenna pattern sweeps past Earth rhythmically, causing signal strength (not frequency) to rise and fall.
T8B10: LEO (Low Earth Orbit) describes satellites with an orbital period of around 100 minutes — close enough to Earth to circle it quickly, which is also why a single pass only lasts several minutes.
T8B11: Anyone is permitted to receive telemetry from an amateur radio satellite — no special authorization needed, since satellite telemetry is meant to be public.
T8B12: Comparing your downlink signal strength to the satellite's own beacon is how you judge your uplink power — if it's about the same as the beacon, your power is in the right range.
T8C01: Radio direction finding is the method used to locate sources of noise interference or jamming, using directional antennas and signal-strength comparisons.
T8C02: A directional antenna is the key piece of gear for a hidden transmitter hunt — it's what lets you narrow down the direction a signal is coming from.
T8C03: Contesting is the operating activity built around contacting as many stations as possible during a specified period.
T8C04: Sending only the minimum information needed for proper identification and the contest exchange is good contest practice — padding transmissions costs everyone time that could go to another contact.
T8C05: A grid locator is a letter-number designator assigned to a geographic location — compact shorthand for "roughly where I am" exchanged in VHF/UHF and contest work.
T8C06: Over-the-air access to IRLP nodes is accomplished using DTMF signals keyed in on your own radio.
T8C07: VoIP (Voice Over Internet Protocol) is a method of delivering voice communications over the internet using digital techniques — the general underlying technology, not amateur-specific by itself.
T8C08: IRLP (Internet Radio Linking Project) is a technique to connect amateur radio systems, such as repeaters, together using the internet.
T8C09: EchoLink is the protocol that enables a station to transmit through a repeater without a radio initiating the connection — a computer or phone app can reach the system directly.
T8C10: Registering your call sign and providing proof of license is required before using EchoLink, since there's no automatic radio-side license check the way there normally would be.
T8C11: A gateway is the general term for an amateur radio station that connects other amateur stations to the internet, regardless of which specific system it runs.
T8D01: CW, RTTY, and packet are all digital communications modes — every choice listed qualifies, which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T8D02: FT8 is a digital mode specifically capable of low signal-to-noise-ratio operation — decoding signals well below what's audible by ear is its whole selling point.
T8D03: APRS can transmit GPS position, short text messages, and weather data — all typical APRS payloads, which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T8D04: NTSC is the specific analog fast-scan color TV signal standard behind amateur "image" mode fast-scan television.
T8D05: Providing real-time tactical digital communications together with a map of reporting stations is APRS's signature application.
T8D06: PSK stands for Phase Shift Keying — encoding information by shifting a carrier's phase rather than switching it on/off or changing its frequency.
T8D07: DMR is a technique for time-multiplexing two digital voice signals onto a single 12.5 kHz repeater channel — the two conversations take turns in rapid alternating time slices.
T8D08: Packet radio transmissions include a header (destination call sign), a checksum (error detection), and ARQ (automatic repeat request) — all of it, which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T8D09: CW is simply another name for a Morse code transmission.
T8D10: Digital mode software (like the WSJT-X suite) supports EME, weak-signal beacons, and meteor scatter — all real specialized activities, which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T8D11: ARQ is an error correction method in which the receiving station detects an error (via checksum) and automatically requests that the data be resent.
T8D12: An amateur radio mesh network is a data network using commercial Wi-Fi equipment (running modified firmware) on amateur bands — repurposed consumer hardware, not custom radio gear.
T9A01: A beam antenna concentrates radiated signal in one direction, trading all-around coverage for stronger signal in the direction it's pointed.
T9A02: Antenna loading means electrically lengthening an antenna by inserting inductors (coils) into the radiating element — useful when a physically full-size antenna won't fit.
T9A03: An antenna's polarization is described by the orientation of the electric field it radiates, which in practice matches the physical orientation of the antenna element.
T9A04: A handheld's short flexible "rubber duck" antenna has low efficiency compared to a full-size quarter-wave antenna — it's electrically shortened, and that shortening costs signal.
T9A05: Shortening a dipole raises its resonant frequency — there's simply less wire for the signal to travel across.
T9A06: Among the antenna types tested, the Yagi offers the greatest gain — it concentrates radiated signal more strongly in one direction than a dipole or vertical.
T9A07: Operating a handheld inside a vehicle without an external antenna reduces signal strength mainly because the vehicle's metal body shields the antenna, acting like a partial cage around it.
T9A08: A 19-inch vertical antenna is a resonant quarter-wave at 2-meter frequencies (146 MHz), following the standard wavelength-to-length relationship.
T9A09: A 5/8-wavelength whip has more gain than a 1/4-wave antenna — it concentrates more of its radiation toward the horizon, where distant stations actually are.
T9A10: A half-wave dipole radiates its strongest signal broadside to the antenna — off to the sides, at right angles to the wire — and almost nothing straight off the ends.
T9A11: Antenna gain is the increase in signal strength in a specified direction compared to a reference antenna — a redistribution of the same total power, not extra power created from nowhere.
T9B01: PL-259, BNC, and Type N connectors should all be carefully taped for weather protection outdoors — none of them are inherently weatherproof on their own, which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T9B02: 50 ohms is the most common characteristic impedance of coaxial cable used in amateur radio, matching what most transmitters and antennas are designed around.
T9B03: Coaxial cable is the most common amateur feed line because it's easy to use and requires few special installation considerations — it doesn't need to be kept clear of nearby metal the way some other feed lines do.
T9B04: An antenna tuner's major function is matching the antenna system's impedance to the transceiver's output impedance, using adjustable inductors and capacitors to cancel out the mismatch.
T9B05: Coax loss increases as frequency increases — the same cable that's efficient on HF loses noticeably more power feeding a UHF antenna.
T9B06: Type N is the more suitable RF connector above 400 MHz, where a PL-259's mechanical design starts introducing more loss and impedance irregularity than a Type N's more precise design.
T9B07: PL-259 connectors are commonly used at HF and VHF frequencies — inexpensive and easy to solder, though not the best performer at very high frequencies.
T9B08: Water in connectors, high SWR, and multiple connectors in a run are all real, independent sources of feed line loss — which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T9B09: A loose connection in the antenna or feed line is a strong sign behind erratic SWR changes — a mechanical fault, not a propagation effect.
T9B10: RG-213 has less loss at a given frequency than RG-58 — the extra bulk is mostly dielectric and shielding that reduces how much signal is absorbed per foot.
T9B11: Air-insulated hardline has the lowest loss of the common feed line types, since it uses air rather than solid plastic as its insulator.
T9B12: SWR (standing wave ratio) is a measure of how well a load — typically the antenna system — is matched to the transmission line feeding it.
T0A01: Shorting a 12-volt battery's terminals together can cause burns, fire, or an explosion — a fully charged battery can deliver an enormous current burst into a dead short, generating heat fast enough to ignite materials or rupture the case.
T0A02: Current through the body can heat tissue, disrupt nerve signals, and cause involuntary muscle contractions all at once — every choice is a real hazard, which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T0A03: In standard US three-wire 120V AC wiring, black insulation indicates the hot conductor — the one that actually carries voltage.
T0A04: A fuse's purpose is to remove power in case of an overload, via a thin element that melts and opens the circuit once current exceeds its rating.
T0A05: Replacing a 5-amp fuse with a 20-amp one lets excessive current flow before anything interrupts it, which can generate enough heat to cause a fire — the wiring downstream was sized for 5 amps of protection, not 20.
T0A06: Three-wire cords/plugs, a common safety ground, and discharging high-voltage capacitors before working inside equipment are all good shock-prevention habits — which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T0A07: A lightning arrester on a coaxial feed line should be installed on a grounded panel near where the feed line enters the building — catching a surge at the entry point before it travels further inside.
T0A08: A fuse or circuit breaker must be installed in series with the hot conductor only — a fuse in the neutral could open while the hot conductor (and everything wired to it) stays fully energized.
T0A09: External ground rods and earth connections should all be bonded together with heavy wire or conductive strap — unbonded ground points can sit at different voltages during a fault, and bonding keeps everything at the same potential.
T0A10: Rapidly charging or discharging an unprotected battery risks overheating or out-gassing — the thermal/chemical stress of pushing current in or out faster than the battery is built to handle.
T0A11: Charge stored in filter capacitors is the hazard immediately after turning a power supply off — the "off" switch stops new charging, but doesn't drain what's already stored.
T0A12: When measuring high voltages, ensure the voltmeter and its leads are actually rated for the voltage present — an underrated meter or probe can arc over, fail, or expose you to that voltage at the point of failure.
T0B01: Ensuring ground wire connections are short and direct is good practice for tower lightning grounding — a lightning surge doesn't follow bends smoothly and can arc across them instead.
T0B02: Climbing an antenna tower requires sufficient training, an approved climbing harness, and appropriate tie-off all at once — every element is required, which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T0B03: It is never safe to climb a tower without a helper or observer present — no exception for height or job simplicity, since there'd be no one to call for help if a fall or medical emergency happened.
T0B04: Looking for and staying clear of overhead electrical wires is a critical safety precaution both while erecting and afterward — contact with power lines is a leading cause of tower-work fatalities.
T0B05: A safety wire through a turnbuckle prevents it from loosening due to vibration — wind constantly shakes a tower, and an unlocked turnbuckle can slowly back itself off.
T0B06: The minimum safe distance from a power line is measured so that if the antenna ever falls, no part of it can come within 10 feet of the power wires — calculated from the antenna's full fallen length, not the tower base.
T0B07: A crank-up tower must not be climbed unless it's fully retracted or mechanical safety locking devices have been installed — an extended crank-up tower can telescope back down unexpectedly under load otherwise.
T0B08: Proper tower grounding uses separate eight-foot ground rods at each tower leg, bonded to the tower and to each other — not a single short rod or a piggyback on a water pipe.
T0B09: Attaching an antenna to a utility pole risks the antenna (or its hardware) contacting high-voltage power lines that share the pole.
T0B10: Sharp bends must be avoided in lightning grounding conductors — a surge can jump (arc) across a sharp bend instead of following the wire, defeating the ground path.
T0B11: Local electrical codes establish grounding requirements for an amateur radio tower or antenna — not FCC Part 97, which is a federal amateur-radio rule, not a grounding code.
T0C01: Radio signals are non-ionizing radiation — they don't carry enough energy per photon to knock electrons out of atoms or break chemical bonds the way X-rays or gamma rays do.
T0C02: Among the frequencies compared, 50 MHz has the lowest (most restrictive) maximum permissible exposure — a consequence of the human body being an unusually efficient absorber of RF energy in roughly that range.
T0C03: Dropping duty cycle from 100% to 50% increases the allowable power density by a factor of 2 — you're only "on" half the time, so you can be twice as intense while on and still average out to the same exposure.
T0C04: The RF field's frequency and power level, the distance from the antenna to the person, and the antenna's radiation pattern all factor into RF exposure together — which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T0C05: Exposure limits vary with frequency because the human body absorbs more RF energy at some frequencies than others — the "safe" power level has to be lower right where absorption peaks.
T0C06: Calculation per FCC OET Bulletin 65, computer modeling, and direct field-strength measurement are all acceptable ways to verify RF exposure compliance — which is why "all these choices" is correct.
T0C07: Touching an antenna during a transmission creates a hazard of RF burn to the skin — RF current concentrates at the point of contact and heats that spot rapidly.
T0C08: Relocating antennas — further from where people spend time, or higher up — is an effective action to reduce RF exposure.
T0C09: Re-evaluating the station whenever anything in the transmitter or antenna system changes is how you make sure it stays in RF-safety compliance — a new antenna, feed line, or power increase can all shift exposure levels.
T0C10: Duty cycle matters for RF exposure because it affects the average exposure over time, not just the instantaneous peak — a transmitter running high power briefly can still average out lower than one running continuously.
T0C11: Duty cycle, for RF exposure purposes, is defined as the percentage of time a transmitter is actually transmitting during the exposure-averaging period.
T0C12: RF radiation doesn't have sufficient energy to cause chemical changes in cells and damage DNA the way ionizing radiation does — its hazard mechanism is heating, not cellular-level damage.
T0C13: The station licensee is responsible for ensuring no person is exposed to RF energy above the FCC's limits — not the FCC itself, and not anyone who happens to wander near the antenna.
